title: Cross-column and append drop UI
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Complete cross-column and append drag-and-drop behavior on the project board: move cards between workflow columns, drop into empty columns, drop onto column backgrounds to append, highlight target columns/drop areas, and keep invalid workflow-column tasks isolated from normal board movement.

User stories addressed:
- User story 2
- User story 5
- User story 6
- User story 16
- User story 17
- User story 21
- User story 32
design: Depends on kanai-jxd.3 so base card drag behavior and same-column placement are already stable before adding broader column targets. Continue modifying the existing board page and hook seams in place. Keep scope to desktop pointer drag-and-drop; mobile layouts should continue to load, but touch-specific DnD is out of scope.
acceptance_criteria:
- Users can drag a task card to a different workflow column and persist the destination column.
- Dropping into an empty column sends a no-neighbor destination and appends deterministically server-side.
- Dropping onto a column background/end-of-column target appends to that column.
- Target columns are highlighted during drag, and empty/end-of-column drop areas have visible styled feedback.
- Tasks with missing or stale workflow column IDs remain in the integrity warning area and are not normal drag/drop targets.
- Focused UI tests verify cross-column rendered behavior, empty/append drop feedback where practical, invalid-task isolation, and standard task navigation remains usable.

title: Same-column card reorder UI
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Complete same-column task-card drag-and-drop on the project board: make the whole card draggable, preserve normal card click navigation, support dropping above or below another task in the same column, and show clear source-card and insertion feedback.

User stories addressed:
- User story 1
- User story 3
- User story 4
- User story 15
- User story 18
- User story 19
- User story 20
- User story 32
design: Depends on kanai-jxd.2 because UI should call the finalized hook move function and use its pending/error state. Modify the existing ProjectBoardPage/board UI in place, preserving existing layout and TanStack Router links. Use focused tests around helper/drop calculation and rendered states rather than full jsdom drag simulation.
acceptance_criteria:
- Users can drag a task card within its current column and drop it above or below another task.
- Drop calculations send before_task_id and after_task_id destination neighbors through the board hook.
- The dragged source card visibly indicates it is being dragged.
- Hovering a task card shows an insertion indicator for top/bottom placement.
- Clicking a task card still opens task details and is not broken by drag setup.
- Dragging is disabled or blocked while the hook reports a pending move.
- Focused UI/hook tests verify rendered order, same-column move destination calculation, insertion feedback where practical, pending disabled state where practical, and continued task-detail navigation without brittle full browser drag simulation.

title: Optimistic move hook with rollback
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Switch the project board state hook to destination-based task moves, with immediate optimistic cache movement, automatic rollback on failed saves, inline move-failure state, server-authoritative refetch after settlement, and single in-flight move protection.

User stories addressed:
- User story 7
- User story 8
- User story 9
- User story 10
- User story 11
- User story 12
- User story 13
- User story 14
- User story 32
design: Depends on the server-owned move API/client wrapper from kanai-jxd.1. Keep the implementation inside the existing board state hook rather than introducing new component modules. The frontend may compute temporary local rank/order to keep optimistic cache sorting stable, but persisted rank values must only come from the backend move workflow.
acceptance_criteria:
- useProjectTaskBoard calls the destination-based task move API rather than generic PATCH rank updates.
- Dropping a task updates cached board order immediately using temporary local ordering only for UI stability.
- A failed move restores the previous cached task list and exposes an inline move error message for the board UI.
- A successful or failed move invalidates/refetches project tasks after settlement.
- While a move is pending, additional move requests are ignored or blocked and dragging can be disabled by the UI.
- Focused hook tests cover optimistic movement, rollback, pending-state behavior, error-message behavior, and post-settlement invalidation.

title: Server-owned task movement contract
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Complete the backend-owned task move contract end-to-end: expose the dedicated PUT move endpoint, accept destination column plus optional before/after neighbors, return the moved task, reject invalid access or ambiguous neighbors, remove client-authored rank from generic create/update, append new tasks server-side, regenerate the OpenAPI client, and update the frontend task API wrapper to call the move workflow.

User stories addressed:
- User story 22
- User story 23
- User story 24
- User story 25
- User story 26
- User story 27
- User story 28
- User story 29
- User story 30
- User story 31
design: This slice establishes the vertical contract used by later UI slices. Build on app/features/tasks/service.py, app/features/tasks/router.py, app/schemas/task.py, existing task repository rank ordering, and api/tests/services/test_task_move_workflow.py. Preserve the existing fractional lexicographic rank algorithm and avoid database migrations. Regenerate the frontend OpenAPI client after backend schema/route changes, then update client/src/api/client/tasks.ts and its tests so later board code does not persist rank through PATCH.
acceptance_criteria:
- PUT /projects/{project_id}/tasks/{task_id}/move accepts destination column_id plus optional before_task_id and after_task_id and returns the moved TaskRead.
- Backend computes and persists ranks for within-column moves, cross-column moves, and no-neighbor append moves.
- Non-adjacent before/after neighbors and neighbors outside the destination column are rejected.
- Project owners and members can move tasks; users without project access are denied.
- Task create and generic update no longer accept client-authored rank values, and creation appends server-side by default.
- Generated OpenAPI client and custom task API wrapper expose the destination-based move request and map the moved task response.
- Targeted backend and frontend API tests cover the route/schema/client contract.

title: PRD: Project board task drag-and-drop
notes: Ralph worker incomplete: epic has no concrete standalone acceptance criteria and referenced issues/prd.md is absent. Branch: ralph/kanai-jxd. Worktree: .worktrees/kanai-jxd. Log: .ralph/logs/kanai-jxd.md

title: Manual prompt contract review
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

Decision needed:
Review and approve the separated worker and merge prompt contracts before the parallel AFK workflow is treated as production-ready. Confirm that worker prompts are limited to isolated issue implementation and read-only issue tracking after assignment, while merge prompts own integration, verification, issue closure, follow-up creation, and cleanup.

User stories addressed:
- User story 53
design: HITL slice because the PRD explicitly calls for manual prompt review rather than unit-testing prompt text. Blocks final end-to-end hardening so the full workflow is not considered complete before prompt contracts are approved.
acceptance_criteria:
- Human reviewer confirms worker prompt responsibilities are focused and auditable.
- Human reviewer confirms merge prompt responsibilities are focused and auditable.
- Reviewer explicitly approves HITL/AFK boundaries and issue-mutation boundaries in the prompt contracts.
- Any requested prompt changes are captured before the final wave loop hardening slice proceeds.
notes: Prompt contract review completed. Fixed worker/merge boundary ambiguity by adding ralph/worker-prompt.md for assigned-issue-only isolated workers, pointing ralph/afk.sh at it, and tightening ralph/merge-prompt.md issue-mutation scope. Verification passed: bash -n ralph/afk.sh ralph/helpers.sh ralph/once.sh; bash ralph/helpers.test.sh. Pending human approval before closure.
close_reason: Approved: worker and merge prompt contracts reviewed and boundaries accepted

title: End-to-end wave loop hardening
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Wire the full parallel AFK loop so each required iteration is one wave plus merge integration, dependent work can unlock between waves, no automatic retry or dry-run mode is introduced, no remote push occurs, existing serial/manual Ralph compatibility is retained, and the finished workflow remains conservative and locally reviewable.

User stories addressed:
- User story 5
- User story 6
- User story 47
- User story 48
- User story 49
- User story 50
- User story 55
design: Final integration slice. Depends on parallel worker execution, merge safety, and prompt contract approval. Keep behavior conservative: local-only automation, clean-checkout guardrails, explicit markers, and no publishing to remotes.
acceptance_criteria:
- The required iteration argument controls the maximum number of AFK waves.
- Each iteration performs issue discovery, worker execution, and merge integration as one wave.
- Newly unblocked dependent work can be picked up in later waves.
- In-progress or failed issues are not retried automatically.
- No dry-run mode and no automatic remote push are added.
- Existing one-off serial/manual Ralph flow remains compatible.
- The final workflow reports no-ready, no-more-work, partial, and failure states clearly.
close_reason: Completed: hardened end-to-end AFK wave loop orchestration

title: Merge safety and partial failure handling
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Harden merge integration for conflicts and partial success. Have the merge agent attempt safe semantic conflict resolution, abort unresolved merges, preserve failed or unmerged worktrees and branches, emit and parse complete/partial/failed merge markers, continue after partial success only when the main checkout is clean, and stop immediately on unsafe merge failure or missing markers.

User stories addressed:
- User story 37
- User story 38
- User story 43
- User story 44
- User story 45
- User story 46
design: Depends on the merge happy path. This slice focuses on conservative failure handling and loop control so integration risk does not compound across waves.
acceptance_criteria:
- Simple merge conflicts may be resolved semantically when safe.
- Unresolved conflicts are aborted without leaving the main checkout in an unsafe merge state.
- Failed or unmerged worktrees and branches are preserved for inspection.
- Complete, partial, failed, and missing merge marker cases are interpreted deterministically.
- Partial merge success allows the next wave only when the main checkout is clean.
- Merge failure or missing merge markers stop the AFK loop immediately.
close_reason: Completed: hardened merge safety for unresolved failures and clean continuation

title: Parallel worker wave execution
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Expand isolated worker execution into a full wave. For each iteration, select up to the configured cap of claimed AFK issues, launch workers concurrently in dedicated worktrees, collect per-worker results, and display concise lifecycle status instead of interleaved worker streams.

User stories addressed:
- User story 1
- User story 5
- User story 8
- User story 20
- User story 29
- User story 30
- User story 31
design: Depends on the single-worker tracer so concurrency is an expansion of a known-good worker contract. Keep scheduling simple: first N ready issues in bd order, bounded by the validated cap.
acceptance_criteria:
- Each iteration launches at most one wave of workers plus later integration.
- Parallelism defaults to four and is capped by the configured positive integer.
- Workers run concurrently in separate worktrees with explicit working paths.
- Terminal output shows concise per-issue lifecycle status rather than raw interleaved worker streams.
- Per-wave and per-issue logs remain available under ignored runtime directories.
close_reason: Completed: added bounded concurrent Ralph worker wave execution

title: Merge agent happy path
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Invoke a dedicated merge-agent sandbox from the main checkout when at least one worker branch is marked complete. Pass a structured wave summary, merge complete branches one at a time with non-fast-forward merge commits, run integrated verification, close issues only after successful merge and verification, create linked follow-up issues from worker output, clean successfully merged artifacts, and avoid remote pushes.

User stories addressed:
- User story 32
- User story 33
- User story 34
- User story 35
- User story 36
- User story 39
- User story 40
- User story 41
- User story 42
- User story 44
- User story 47
- User story 54
- User story 55
design: Can start once the single-worker tracer produces a complete branch and structured wave summary. This establishes the integration ownership boundary: workers commit isolated branches, the merge agent owns merge, verification, issue closure, follow-up creation, and successful cleanup.
acceptance_criteria:
- Merge agent runs only when at least one worker produced a complete committed branch.
- Merge agent receives issue IDs, branches, worktrees, logs, base commit, markers, and incomplete/failure summaries.
- Complete branches are merged one at a time using non-fast-forward merge commits.
- Integrated verification runs before issue closure.
- Issues are closed only after successful merge and verification.
- Linked follow-up issues are created from structured worker follow-up output.
- Successfully merged worktrees and branches are cleaned, and no remote push is performed.
close_reason: Completed: added Ralph merge-agent happy path orchestration

title: Single isolated worker tracer bullet
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Run one claimed AFK issue through an isolated worker path. Create a deterministic branch and worktree from the current clean base commit, launch opencode-sandbox against the explicit worktree path, pass assigned issue JSON and recent commits, capture per-issue logs, require an actual commit plus explicit completion marker, and preserve failed or incomplete worktrees.

User stories addressed:
- User story 13
- User story 14
- User story 15
- User story 16
- User story 17
- User story 18
- User story 19
- User story 20
- User story 21
- User story 22
- User story 23
- User story 24
- User story 25
- User story 26
- User story 27
- User story 28
- User story 29
- User story 31
- User story 52
- User story 53
design: Depends on issue discovery and helper parsing. This is the first end-to-end tracer through selection, worktree setup, worker prompt contract, logging, marker parsing, and commit verification, but intentionally limits execution to one worker before introducing concurrency.
acceptance_criteria:
- A single assigned issue runs in a deterministic branch and worktree derived from the issue ID.
- Existing branches or worktrees for the issue are skipped rather than overwritten.
- Worker logs are written under ignored runtime directories and include full worker output.
- Worker success requires a commit on the worker branch and an explicit complete marker.
- Missing markers, nonzero exits, and incomplete markers are treated as non-success while preserving evidence.
- Worker prompt instructs the worker to treat issue tracking as read-only after assignment.
close_reason: Completed: added single isolated Ralph worker tracer bullet

title: AFK issue discovery and claiming
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Implement dependency-ready AFK issue discovery and assignment. Query bd for AFK-ready work, exclude HITL work, preserve bd output order, claim selected issues before worker launch, skip failed claims, and handle no-ready/no-more-work states without launching a sandbox.

User stories addressed:
- User story 2
- User story 3
- User story 4
- User story 10
- User story 11
- User story 12
- User story 48
- User story 54
design: Depends on the helper baseline for validation and issue selection primitives. Keep issue mutation centralized in the orchestrator at assignment time; workers must receive assigned issue data rather than picking work themselves.
acceptance_criteria:
- Ralph selects only AFK-labeled ready issues and excludes HITL issues.
- Readiness is based on unresolved blockers rather than the literal absence of dependency records.
- Selected issues are claimed before any worker process is spawned.
- Failed claims are skipped without assigning duplicate work.
- No-ready and no-more-work outcomes are represented without invoking opencode-sandbox.
close_reason: Completed: added AFK ready issue assignment and no-work handling

title: Validation and helper baseline
description:
Parent PRD: issues/prd.md

What to build:
Introduce a testable Ralph helper layer for deterministic orchestration decisions while preserving the existing one-off runner path. Cover positive integer validation, required tool checks, clean-checkout checks, worker and merge marker parsing, and wave-summary construction.

User stories addressed:
- User story 6
- User story 7
- User story 8
- User story 9
- User story 50
- User story 51
- User story 52
design: Foundation slice. Keep the helper layer small and stable so later shell orchestration can delegate parsing, validation, marker interpretation, and summary formatting without over-testing live opencode-sandbox behavior. Do not add remote push behavior or break existing serial/manual Ralph usage.
acceptance_criteria:
- Iteration count validation accepts only positive integers and rejects missing, zero, negative, and non-numeric input.
- Parallelism cap validation defaults to four and rejects invalid configured values before mutating state.
- Required tool and clean-checkout helper behavior is covered without invoking live autonomous agents.
- Worker marker, merge marker, and wave-summary helpers have focused deterministic tests.
- ralph/once.sh remains compatible with the current serial/manual flow.
close_reason: Completed: added deterministic Ralph helper validation and parsing baseline
